feed.title: Today's Darling
feed.subtitle: Daily essays by Shigesato Itoi from 1101.com, translated to English.

entry.title: A match before the lord and sweet red bean soup.
entry.content:
In the old days, there were far more period dramas in manga and movies, so a kind of samurai era literacy took root even in children. Words like "blackened teeth," "vendetta," "masterless samurai," or that old saying about a samurai using a toothpick with pride even when he hasn't eaten, most grade schoolers probably knew roughly what they all meant.

Among all that familiar period drama knowledge was something called the "tournament before the lord." Swordmasters would fight matches in the presence of the shogun or a feudal lord. There'd be a well groomed samurai of decent rank, and a shabby masterless drifter smirking to himself, and someone wielding a strange weapon like a chained sickle. It seemed to be run as a bracket, with the strong ones fighting their way to the top. You'd win, show off your strength, and earn the lord's recognition. A chance at official service as a reward, honor, money. There must have been real reasons to fight in earnest.

In any case, it's fighting, but it isn't war. It's a "match" premised on being held before the eyes of "someone important." How you're seen, how you're judged, that becomes the whole point.

It's an event I only know from manga and movies, but when I watch the "debates within the media" of our current age, I can't help feeling that this is a gathering of "swordmasters" (self proclaimed or otherwise) holding a "tournament before the lord." The difference is that what used to be done before the "lord" is now done before the "masses."

They don't use real blades that would leave the opponent dead, but they swing wooden swords and bamboo practice swords and imitation blades with all their might. They let out battle cries too, and there are opening declarations, cheering sections, strategies, bluffs, and gambits. In the end, is being acknowledged as "you're strong!" what counts as the final victory?

Voices echoing "it is I," "no, it is I," and the more the arena fills with noise, the more the "lords" (the masses) multiply. In the old days they may have boasted of their skill with the sword, but these days, what strength is it exactly that they're all competing over?

Thank you again for coming to Hobonichi today. Tournaments before the lord are all well and good, but personally I'm more of a fan of sweet red bean soup served before me.
entry.summary: Modern media debates function like historical "tournaments before the lord," where commentators compete for public recognition and validation using rhetorical rather than physical weapons, raising the question of what substantive "strength" they're actually demonstrating.

entry.title: I still don't really understand.
entry.content:
I feel like I've always kept a certain distance from things labeled "love stories" or "romance films." A world of men and women falling in love, loving, parting, and weeping, that was never something I sought out to enjoy in novels or movies.

A long time ago, we happened to have the pamphlet for Zhang Yimou's film "The Road Home" lying around the house. I was admiring the cover, thinking how lovely it was, when I asked the boss of the household, "Was this any good?" and got a curt reply: "Not your kind of thing." That irritated me just enough to go watch it, and wasn't it wonderful? Oh, it was good, I cried and cried.

So even I can't quite make sense of myself. I absolutely love those old pop songs about falling for someone, being smitten, breaking up, weeping. And with dramas I've drifted into watching almost by accident, I find myself tearing up right along with the romantic bits, more often than I'd care to admit. Wait, do I actually like this stuff? Do I?

Lately it was "Everyone's Fighting Their Own Worthless Self." That twelfth episode, or was it, had romance running all through it, and there I was late at night, rooting hard for the lovers.

And then, though I can't quite recall what set it off, I watched a film called "We Made a Beautiful Bouquet." This one has an unusual shape to it too, but really, isn't it a beautifully made love story? I watched it closely, every minute of it.

Riding that momentum from "Bouquet," I ended up watching "The Moon on the Flatland" by the same director, Nobuhiro Doi. Sakai Masato is in it, and I had plenty of reasons to want to see it. Ah, and this one is like a romance that comes after romance, the kind of story that must be irresistible to anyone who has lived a fair stretch of life. Once again I watched it carefully, and let my tear ducts loosen.

I'm not keeping love stories at arm's length at all, am I. Maybe everyone loves a tale of one person being drawn to another. So why did I ever think I didn't much care for them? Now that I'm not going to the theater, perhaps romance films have simply become easier to watch?

Plenty remains unclear, but here's what I'm thinking now: maybe I'll read Goethe's "The Sorrows of Young Werther." Perhaps I'll pick it as the material for my "one-person book club."

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. When someone asks, I have never once said that I like love stories.
entry.summary: The author discovers through recent films and songs that they actually do enjoy romance stories, contrary to their long-held belief, and is surprised to find themselves emotionally invested in love narratives they once avoided. This realization prompts them to reconsider their relationship with the romance genre and consider reading classic romantic literature.

entry.title: It's summer, so are you eating somen and stuff?
entry.content:
In the morning, not long after waking, I can go without eating anything and feel perfectly fine. So people might take me for someone with a weak appetite. But the truth is, I'll get up, get ready to go out first thing, and head off to eat some ramen I've had my eye on. Apparently, to the boss of the household, this has long been considered "a frightening thing." I only just found this out.

I don't think of ramen as some light little meal. And yet, eating ramen right after waking isn't a strain for me at all. Honestly, I'd be fine with a bowl of grilled eel over rice for breakfast. Tempura over rice, pork cutlet over rice, I never think, "It's morning, better not." Well, I might pass on yakiniku, but if someone said, "There's a great place for morning yakiniku, want to go?" I'd answer, "Morning yakiniku, is that a thing? Sounds good, let's go."

I have absolutely no sense that I've got an iron stomach. But the idea that breakfast should be something light, or that on a hot summer day I ought to have somen noodles, those feelings have almost never come over me. I love light meals, and I love somen too, so I'll eat them gladly, morning or summer heat be damned.

What really exasperated the boss, apparently, was the day we had a reservation at the sushi place, and even though I had a fever, I said "I'm going" and ate just like always. I do remember that. I had a fever and felt a bit unsteady, but the usual delicious sushi was delicious in the usual way.

Come to think of it, back when I was young, I once said that even with a fever I could eat eel, and the president at the time treated me to some. It's not that I have a strong stomach. It's just that what I can eat, I can eat.

It's morning, or it's summer, or the feasts have been piling up, sure, I could trot out those excuses if I wanted to. Society tends to think along those lines, and for the most part I go along with it in my daily life.

But here's a thought. Morning is when you're hungriest, so why not eat whatever you like? And summer wears you out, doesn't it, so shouldn't you build up your strength? All right, all right, I'll go along with it, somen or hiyamugi, whatever you say.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today too. And when I eat ramen with too much grease in it, I'm also the guy who gets an upset stomach.
entry.summary: The narrator challenges social conventions about when and what one should eat, arguing that appetite and enjoyment, rather than time of day or circumstance, should dictate food choices. Though willing to accommodate others' expectations, they ultimately believe that eating what genuinely satisfies you—whether ramen at breakfast or sushi with a fever—is perfectly reasonable.

entry.title: In praise of beanbags
entry.content:
Because I have my doubts about my own strength of character, let me get ahead of myself and offer up something that sounds like a comedy duo's stage name: "Easier Said Than Done." That said, honestly, more than I expected, this whole business of juggling beanbags is wonderful.

What I really want to master is the three-beanbag technique they call the "cascade," but rather than reaching for something that extravagant, even with one bag or two, once you simply start doing something, anything, you realize right away just how remarkable it is.

Off the top of my head, here are the benefits. One, your gaze lifts upward. Two, naturally, your field of vision widens. Three, the muscles and nerves in your arms come alive. Four, your fingertips get to work. Five, you exercise your left hand, which usually goes dull from disuse. Six, your shoulder blades start moving properly. That sort of thing comes to mind.

The day after practicing a little too enthusiastically, I actually had quite a bit of muscle soreness. It seems to have worked those so-called inner muscles too.

If you think a bit more deeply about the benefits, the fact that you're "using the predicting part of the brain" is probably significant as well. When you juggle, you're moving your hands by anticipating the next instant. You can tell this is true from how tired it makes your nerves, or your brain, or whatever it is.

Beyond that, the fact that it feels "pretty difficult, but like something I can actually do" is very convenient. If a "problem set" is like this, both beginners and veterans can play with it. That, I think, is why it's easy to keep practicing without giving up.

You can do it in almost any spare moment, in almost any place, and it has hardly any of the dangerous elements that come with sports. And there's no set order to thinking about it and trying it out.

Ordinarily, our lives are spent with heads bowed and backs hunched, sitting in a chair, staring fixedly at a narrow little display. We barely move anything except our eyes and fingertips. Juggling beanbags is probably the exact opposite of all that.

Incidentally, my own set of tools was five bags for under a thousand yen. So there you have it, my report on the all-around wonders of the humble beanbag. ♫

Then again, there's a version of me who can't stick with something even when it's obviously good for him. There's a not-insignificant chance I'll have completely forgotten about it a week from now.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too.

Juggling really is the finest hobby an ordinary person could ask for.
entry.summary: The essay celebrates juggling beanbags as a remarkably simple yet comprehensive physical and mental practice that counters our sedentary, screen-focused lives by engaging the whole body, sharpening the mind, and remaining accessible to anyone regardless of skill level. Though the author acknowledges his tendency to abandon hobbies, he maintains that juggling's ease, affordability, and profound benefits make it an ideal pursuit for ordinary people.

entry.title: Wait, ramen, fried rice, and a love like a bouquet.
entry.content:
When you're chasing someone, you shout "Stop!" at the person you're after. Of course, shouting "Stop" doesn't mean they'll actually stop, and shouting "Stop" doesn't make you run any faster either. So I found myself wondering all over again whether it might be better not to shout "Stop!", and the answer, in the end, is that you're better off shouting it.

For one thing, it has the effect of announcing to everyone around you the simple fact that "a chase is currently underway." Once you've done that, the person being chased draws attention. It becomes harder for them to slip away and hide, which makes them easier to catch.

And there's one more thing. When someone yells "Stop!" at you, I think it genuinely does increase the odds, even a little, that the person running will actually stop. However determined they are to keep running forever, that's a hard thing to pull off. Sooner or later, somewhere along the way, they might just give up. As a word to coax out that surrender, shouting "Stop!" beats staying silent.

So I've decided that someday, when I find myself chasing after someone who's done something wrong, I'll shout "Stop!" without any embarrassment.

On a related note, what about the way villains in TV dramas say "Die!" as they reach for a weapon? I can't see what effect it's supposed to have, and it only makes the crime and the punishment heavier. Saying it seems to do no good whatsoever.

・Yesterday, I made the "ramen fried rice" I'd promised earlier I would eventually make. Fried rice with the usual ramen toppings mixed in: char siu, bamboo shoots, and green onion. Since it's really just fried rice with ramen elements folded in, it didn't feel especially fancy, but it was delicious. And of course you need egg.

・I finally got around to watching the film "We Made a Beautiful Bouquet." I'd made up my mind, without ever seeing it, that it was probably one of those movies where young people fall in love and then one of them gets sick, so I'd skipped it. It was nothing like that at all. Truly, love really is like a bouquet. I wonder if young people watched this without flinching. They're tough. As for us older folks, we can get through it, though maybe with a few tears.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today too. Friday is the day to freely make the most of your own time.
entry.summary: Shouting "Stop!" when chasing someone is more effective than silence because it alerts others to the pursuit and psychologically increases the likelihood the person will eventually surrender, making it worth doing despite seeming futile.

entry.title: You're already a little better.
entry.content:
I often think of what to write here for the next day while I'm in the bath. To put it a little more precisely, that's when some "little idea," the kind that makes me want to write something, tends to come to me. Today was the same. While I was soaking in the tub, I started thinking, "Come to think of it, I often hit on little ideas while I'm in the bath. Let me think about that a bit more and write about it."

This reminds me of something else. When you're at the hair salon getting your hair cut, you're really just sitting there doing nothing, and yet there's a sense of fulfillment, as if you've accomplished something. Your hair gets shaped up, and you come out "a little bit better." All I did was sit in front of a mirror, but I'd spent that time becoming "a little bit better."

With the bath, it's the same. You soak in the tub, let out a contented sigh, and just by savoring the moment you've taken care of the task of bathing. It looks like you're doing nothing, but the thing that needs doing is getting done. Here too, I'm spending time becoming "a little bit better," so beyond that, I'm free to do whatever I like. The premise is already in place, after all: I'm already a little bit better. That sense of ease is what gets my mind working better than usual. The "thinking" that doubles as my usual "getting a little better" task really does tend to turn out more interesting.

There are quite a few people who say ideas come to them while they're out strolling around, and this works the same way. First of all, they're "walking, moving forward," so they have the ease of knowing they can do anything on top of that. Just by moving forward, it feels "productive."

Now, for today's piece. If I were to wrap it up in some suitably respectable way, I suppose it would come to this: when you're tackling something "difficult," it helps to have something "easy" secured first.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Yesterday I took up otedama, the traditional beanbag game. It's a pastime full of little discoveries.
entry.summary: The essay reveals that mundane, effortless activities like bathing, haircuts, or walks paradoxically free the mind for creative thinking by fulfilling basic self-care needs, allowing deeper mental work to flourish with ease. By securing something simple and productive first, we create the psychological comfort necessary for tackling more difficult or creative challenges.

entry.title: Remembering "creating customers" once again.
entry.content:
It was a quarter of a century ago now. I would stuff a bag full of mystery novels and go spend the New Year's holiday on Bali. Poolside, in my room, in the little pavilion out in the garden, for five whole days I did nothing but read. I kept this up for several years running.

But one year, for reasons I no longer recall, a business book by Drucker had found its way in among the mysteries. I'd chosen it and packed it myself, of course, but only in the spirit of "maybe I'll give this a quick look," nothing more.

I started reading it in that same casual mood, in between my usual, familiar mysteries, and wouldn't you know it, the thing turned out to be more interesting than the mysteries. I found myself not even wanting to put it down at mealtimes.

Looking back on it now, I couldn't tell you what exactly I understood, or what I took away from it. These days I can only guess at what I was feeling back then. But there is one line I have always remembered, one I still bring up in conversation.

"There is only one valid definition of business purpose: to create a customer."

There is only one valid definition of business purpose: to create a customer.

"Creating a customer." It stays interesting even if you phrase it a little differently. "Bringing a market into being." "Can you win someone's loyal patronage?" "Can you create a customer who wasn't there before?"

It doesn't have to be some intensely businesslike business, either. You could say the same thing to someone doing comedy. Rather than fighting over customers, you bring customers into existence. And that means a new relationship beginning, and beyond that, it means new sources of enjoyment multiplying.

I used to think of work as something rather dangerous, the sort of thing captured in old sayings like "the moment a man steps outside, he has seven enemies" or "sharp enough to steal the eye from a galloping horse." But "creating a customer," now that sounds like it could be fun.

The times are one long succession of turning points, large and small. Yet however things change, I don't think "creating a customer" ever will. Even with the storm of AI looming right there in front of us, the work remains the same: creating a customer.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Even to an amateur like me who knows nothing about management, this felt like something I could truly understand.
entry.summary: A chance encounter with a business book revealed a transformative idea: that the true purpose of any work is not merely to compete for existing customers, but to create new ones—a principle that remains timeless and universally applicable, from business to art to life itself.

entry.title: Idle musings that started with a dog's teeth
entry.content:
My dog Buiko doesn't mind having her teeth brushed. Maybe she actually hates it, but she puts up with it. So every day she gets a good brushing. And the one doing the brushing, my boss around the house, does it often and does it well.

Even so, plaque builds up, and she still gets cavities and gum disease. Our previous dog, Bouillon, was the same breed, but might have had slightly tougher teeth. If I recall, we did have her plaque removed under general anesthesia, but I think we brushed her teeth a little less often. Buiko gets far better dental care, yet the number of teeth pulled because of cavities or gum disease is higher for her.

Animals don't brush their own teeth. The most they do is chew on something hard now and then. But does that mean they never get cavities? Of course they do. I imagine there are plenty of animals out there with cavities and gum disease.

Long ago, elderly humans lost a lot of teeth too. Dentures were a common joke in manga and the like, but in an era when dentures were a luxury, the teeth must have simply stayed gone.

I haven't asked any AI about this, so I'm just going on instinct, but dinosaurs and all the other animals must have had bad teeth. Although writing it out like that, I'm starting to lose confidence. I suspect they just didn't live long enough for their teeth to fall out.

For creatures in the wild, a single injury or a single illness makes the rest of life hard to survive, so they probably don't make it to the next injury or illness. It seems unlikely there's a pack that keeps nursing a tiger that can no longer move. And when the group moves on to the next place, no one's going to carry it. In the natural world, it's less a case of "one ailment keeps you healthy" and more "one ailment and you're dead."

When you think about it, a dog that lives long enough for its teeth to go bad is, after all, a dog that's part of human society. And for us humans, being able to grumble about our health, "this hurts, that's giving out," is itself proof that we're alive. To be able to live while patching ourselves up as we go, now that's an amazing civilization.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Patching up here, papering over there, I'm doing just fine myself.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how chronic health problems like dental disease are actually a luxury of civilization—a sign that we and our pets live long enough and safely enough to survive ailments that would kill wild animals, transforming our complaints about aging bodies into evidence of a remarkable achievement.

entry.title: To you who have paused, a story about 300 monkeys.
entry.content:
People you meet as an adult, there are many whose birthplace and upbringing you never come to know. If you met them through work, you can imagine a certain amount about their past, but some encounters happen closer to pure chance. H.H. was one of those acquaintances.

It wasn't as though we were especially close, but he was the sort of engaging, likable person everyone warms to. Roughly once every couple of years he'd turn up with some rare coffee from who-knows-where as a gift and fill me in on how he'd been, though for the past several years I'd heard nothing. He was also unreasonably good at karaoke.

About a year ago, he got in touch after a long silence. "Monkeys are truly wonderful." That, delivered with total absorption, was the sum of everything he had come to tell me.

Deep in the mountains of a certain place in Japan that everyone would recognize, H.H. owns an enormous mountain cabin. A giant mountain cabin sounds like a contradiction in terms, but apparently he'd put the building up intending it as a factory, so its size had a certain logic to it. And yet it wasn't a factory, it was a mountain cabin.

The thing is, in that cabin, he says he's doing important work. He's a bit vague about what the work actually is, but he insists it's "nothing bad, nothing that runs afoul of the law or morality." He also says it "varies quite a lot depending on the time of year, the season, and economic conditions," but it seems to be a matter of concentrated, fine handwork.

"The local monkeys do all of it for me."

They don't live there, but some three hundred monkeys, he says, commute to H.H.'s cabin. They work diligently and industriously, eat their supper, and go home. The menu is something H.H. cooks up with all his heart in an enormous pot, and the monkeys, he claims, come to this "factory" because they want to eat it. Can this be true? Is he really the president of a company employing three hundred monkeys?

"For work that's simple but requires patience," he says, "monkeys are better suited to it than humans." He even showed me a photograph of monkeys peeling chestnuts inside the giant cabin.

Having written this tall tale, I'm off to bed for the night.

Thank you again for coming to Hobonichi today. It's a daily thing, after all, so I have to find ways to keep myself from getting bored.
entry.summary: A man reconnects with a casual acquaintance who has retreated to a mountain cabin in Japan, where he claims to employ three hundred commuting monkeys to perform intricate handwork in exchange for home-cooked meals. The essay blurs the line between whimsical fiction and reality, celebrating the eccentric and imaginative ways people reinvent their lives away from ordinary society.

entry.title: It's the sun we see every day, but...
entry.content:
Everyone knows about the sun. For us humans, isn't it the most famous thing there is? And why wouldn't it be? It's impossibly far away, yet we see its face every single day. Here in Japan, people call it "O-hisama," treating it with affection, with gratitude, even with reverence.

Thanks to the sun, or because of the sun, this and that keep happening, endlessly. Life itself was built on the energy the sun provides, and it's through our relationship with the sun that years and days exist at all. Since ancient times, no, perhaps even before human history, back in the days of our ape-like ancestors, the sun has surely been thought of as something special, an object of awe and worship.

These days we hear a lot about how long humanity can even remain on Earth, a growing sense of crisis about the future, and talk of whether or not we'll relocate to other planets. But there's a future far beyond even all of that.

Once you get this far, it becomes little more than a game played with numbers, but apparently, around five billion years from now, the hydrogen at the sun's core, the fuel for the nuclear fusion that makes it shine, will begin to run out, and the sun will swell dramatically into what's called a "red giant." It'll become a colossal star, roughly a hundred times its current size, and it will most likely swallow the Earth whole.

At that point, we can't exactly go on calling it "O-hisama" and taking it easy. Whether humanity flees or perishes hardly matters, because neither Earth nor Mars will be habitable at all. There's nothing to be done. True, it's so far off it's almost laughable. But the old saying that you shouldn't assume something will last forever applies to more than just your parents and your money. Looked at on a grand enough scale, even our eternity has its limits.

The sun formed, and then Earth formed after it, about 4.6 billion years ago. When roughly that same span of time has passed from now, both the sun as we know it and the Earth as we know it will meet their end. When you look up at the sun with even a little of that in mind, something almost like faith wells up inside you. After all, they say we humans are made of the very stuff of ancient stars.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. How to put it, I suppose I've got a bit of a soft spot for the sun. A real fan, you might say.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on humanity's intimate dependence on the sun throughout history while contemplating the cosmic perspective that even our star and planet will eventually perish in billions of years, inspiring both gratitude and humility toward our finite existence.

entry.title: Here's what people who can't sleep should try, apparently.
entry.content:
I like finding a hypothesis, a little "maybe it's like this?", and then spending a while turning it over in my mind and testing it out.

Right now, to put it broadly, I've been thinking about how "your subjective take matters" far more than I ever imagined. Even when the exact same thing happens, everything can veer off in a completely different direction depending on the "subjectivity" of the person taking it in. The facts are identical, the cast of characters, the time, the place, all of it the same, and yet depending on "how you feel about it, how you think about it," you end up connected to an utterly, utterly different future.

Someone who can hold a "nice subjectivity" probably moves closer to happiness, while someone with the habit of a "bad subjectivity" seems bound for a rough time. Of course, I'm no fortune-teller, so I can't say for certain. But I get the feeling there might be some kind of training you could do to strengthen that "nice subjectivity." The same way you can train up your "core" muscles. And so, at the moment, this is the idea that's all the rage inside my head.

Now then, one thing I've been experimenting with pretty much every day is the practice of falling asleep without taking a "sleeping aid." Like most old folks, at a certain point I got bad at sleeping. Getting into bed and not being able to drift off, waking up over and over, these unwelcome tendencies had been with me for ages, so I took the quick route and started using "medicine that makes it easier to fall asleep." With it, sleep came easily, and I got hooked.

But then I learned that fretting too much over "not being able to sleep" is itself the problem, that simply lying down with your eyes closed already counts as rest, and I decided to try, as much as I could, to think of it that way. That's not to say the feeling of "I'd hate it if I couldn't sleep" has been wiped away entirely, mind you.

I made myself believe, subjectively, that "even if I can't sleep, it's fine," and I get into bed with enough ease to think, if I still can't sleep after a while, I'll figure out my next move then. And so, it's not that I drop off instantly, but before long, sleep comes.

I warm up in the bath, and I go to bed earlier than I used to. And I don't let the sleeplessness get to me. I just think, "Come on, bring it." With this, somehow, it's been going well for nearly a month now. I'd like to keep it up until I hit a wall or simply get bored of it.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today too. A "nice subjectivity" really might be something you can pick up with a little practice, don't you think?
entry.summary: The author explores how our subjective perspective—how we interpret and feel about events—shapes our future more than the events themselves, and demonstrates this principle through successfully reframing his relationship with sleep by adopting a more accepting mindset rather than fighting against sleeplessness.

entry.title: What I'm thinking about on July 11th
entry.content:
I come to Kyoto around this time of year. It's become a kind of ritual, so here I am again. It has also come to feel like a signal that summer is beginning. For some reason, in this country, or perhaps just for me, summer is the season when I think most about the dead.

We remember the day a person was born. We call it a birthday. And then others remember the day that person died. That is called the anniversary of their death. It exists, I suppose, so that we won't forget that a life was lived. Every year, I find myself thinking, another new death-day has been added. A date that didn't exist while a person was alive is born the moment they die. Both birthdays and death-days remain until no one remembers them anymore, and once there's no one left to remember, they too disappear. You could say that every person carries two dates that are uniquely their own. As for me, I don't yet find myself wanting a death-day of my own, but I understand that one day I'll have one. "A good death-day," now there's a rather nice concept.

That reminds me, I got a letter from Kuma-chan, who passed away in April. No, he didn't come back to life to write it. Someone close to him, someone who was there on the day he died, sent me a letter that Kuma-chan had asked them to pass along. To put it simply, it read like an imagined conversation happening right then and there, something like, "Well, the time's finally come." When the moment truly arrived, when he thought, "So this is it," he must have decided, send that guy a letter. I felt grateful, receiving it.

We'd made plans for me to go see him around that time, but Kuma-chan's condition worsened and we had to postpone. Whether we'd managed to meet or not, I think it was a good death-day. I don't know whether everyone gets to have such a good death-day, but living with that intention seems like a fine thing. And come to think of it, even if a day was full of sorrow at the time, there are cases where you can remake it later into a good death-day.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. Iwata-san, Sobu-chan, Kuma-chan, you may be dead, but you're still alive.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on death and remembrance through the author's annual pilgrimage to Kyoto, exploring how the anniversaries of our deaths—like our birthdays—give structure to memory and allow us to honor those who have passed by living with intention and gratitude.

entry.title: Singing in front of about a hundred people.
entry.content:
Take Akiko Yano, for example. If you asked whether she'd been performing at Orchard Hall since way back, the answer would be not at all. She was doing live shows at Janjan in Shibuya, perfectly ordinary ones. Packing in fewer than a hundred people, crammed tight together. Tatsuro Yamashita was playing at a live house in, I think it was Koenji. That was fewer than a hundred people too, of course. When RC Succession relaunched with that style of theirs, they were playing a live house in Shibuya that held fewer than a hundred. "If every one of you here brought just one more friend, we'd double this audience!" Cleary was how he put it, very concrete, very specific.

Whether someone was famous, whether they were popular or not, the difference probably only amounted to whether you needed a numbered ticket to get in. Every artist was well known to the people who loved them, but filling a large venue on your own wasn't something that happened easily. If you traced the timeline, sure, this song came out in this year, the lineup changed here, it's all there in the record, and the fans know it. But none of it was really creating some massive wave.

Eiichi Ohtaki kept putting out album after album from his studio in Fussa, but the only people buying them were the fans who were eagerly waiting for each one. I realize now, belatedly, that there weren't actually all that many of them. A hundred people, two hundred, a thousand at most. Music was being written and performed in front of numbers you could see, numbers you could count. What you might call handcrafted music was simply the norm.

That got multiplied and scaled up, and we arrived at this era where mega-hits are the thing everyone talks about. But even today's massive hitmakers must have started out standing in front of a hundred people. At the starting line, everyone's the same.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi today, too. What matters is being able to sing joyfully in front of a small audience.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how legendary Japanese musicians like Akiko Yano and Tatsuro Yamashita began their careers performing in small venues for intimate audiences of fewer than a hundred people, arguing that regardless of eventual fame, all artists start from the same humble beginning where direct connection with devoted fans, rather than massive popularity, is what truly matters.

entry.title: An exhausting but fun last night.
entry.content:
By the way, I really did eat tonkatsu yesterday. It was every bit as delicious as I'd imagined. There's something genuinely wonderful about having tomorrow's pleasure already picked out.

So. Right now, I'm pretty worn out. Like a rag that's been wrung tight and left completely dry.

Last night, I produced a pilot version of something I'd been turning over in my mind for a long time, wondering if it might work. For me, it's actually quite rare to come up with an idea myself and then say, "All right, I'll do it." Mostly because I already know going in that it's going to be exhausting.

To put it simply, it's a live event where I read aloud from a book that people think they've read but actually haven't, and as I go, I insert my own questions and reactions along the way. You might call it a "one-man book club." Taking something I normally do alone, reading, and doing it in front of people who are listening.

N-ta-san said, "So it's kind of like a video game playthrough, but for books." I told him that might not be far off.

While reading, when I think, "Oh, that's lovely," or "How on earth did the author manage to write it this way," I say it out loud. All those things you normally do in silence, alone. You could also call it "storytime for adults."

And one thing that's pretty important: no preparation, no research. I face the book completely bare. In the spirit of something that's been my personal motto lately: "Things only really begin once everyone can see you're a fool." When I do that, the people listening don't have to put on airs either. People who read a lot and people who never read at all, both are welcome. That's the kind of book club this is.

Since it was a pilot, we only streamed it internally, as a test to see whether people would enjoy it. In the end, I was tired but it was genuinely interesting. The response from those who watched was quite positive, so we may end up making it available to the public. Ninety minutes of nonstop reading and talking. Is this really something an old man should be doing?

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today. The book I chose for the reading was, of all things, by that master of prose, Shiga Naoya.
entry.summary: The author describes launching a new "one-man book club" concept where he reads aloud from a book while sharing unfiltered reactions and questions, deliberately unprepared and vulnerable to create an authentic space where all readers—experienced or not—feel welcome. Despite the exhaustion, the pilot event resonated with audiences, suggesting that turning solitary reading into a shared, genuine performance can be both creatively fulfilling and connecting.

entry.title: Do you look forward to morning coming when you wake up?
entry.content:
You know when you get into bed, right before you fall asleep, and you think, "When I wake up, morning will be here. I can't wait." Have you ever actually fallen asleep feeling that way? I can imagine it, sure, but I don't think I've ever really experienced it. Well, maybe I have? Maybe I've just forgotten, and there were mornings I actually looked forward to waking up to. If so, I'd love to remember what that felt like. Because wouldn't that be incredible? That feeling of drifting off to sleep excited for morning to come. I bet your face would have a little smile on it. And then you'd slip right into sleep, just like that.

Isn't there some way to try it? What kind of tomorrow would make you look forward to it that much? I can't quite come up with anything, because it's been so long since I've felt that way. Even if it's make-believe, even if it's a joke, I want to know what that tastes like. O grant this old turtle-hearted soul of mine the joy of looking forward to morning.

I mean, it's not like I dread my everyday life or anything. But going to bed looking forward to tomorrow morning, and then waking up with a feeling of genuine happiness. Isn't that more desirable than just about anything? I think "hope" is something that lives inside that feeling. If the word "hope" shows up written in some kind of project proposal, it always feels a little forced, and it's hard to get excited about it.

Come to think of it, I once came up with a line of dialogue. A guy says, "Tomorrow, I'm having tonkatsu." He goes out of his way to tell a coworker during lunch break, in a low voice. He must be so excited about it, just counting the hours. I think that counts as hope, too. I hope he remembers it again right before he falls asleep. Maybe I'll try falling asleep tonight thinking about tomorrow's tonkatsu.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. Oh, and people who are really into their fan activities, they probably look forward to tomorrow all the time, don't they.
entry.summary: The essay explores how genuine anticipation for tomorrow—whether it's something as simple as looking forward to eating tonkatsu—represents a form of hope that many of us have lost touch with, and reflects on the quiet joy of falling asleep excited about what morning will bring.

entry.title: Just seeing a lot of things, and other matters.
entry.content:
Punch, the little monkey at the Ichikawa Zoological and Botanical Garden, may well have become a full-fledged international star by now. He's grown up quite a bit, getting closer to what you might call a young man. These days, it seems he even looks after the newer baby monkeys, the ones born not long ago.

Thanks to Punch, I've spent far more time watching the monkeys on their hill than I ever have before. Never in my life have I spent this much time gazing at a monkey enclosure. I think there's something truly wonderful about simply watching, a lot, without studying or trying to learn anything from it.

When it comes to the things we love, we just watch. A lot. Not for any purpose. Just to watch. Because the watching itself feels good. We don't only look from straight on. We look from a distance too. We look from behind, where they can't see us looking back. We watch them when they're still, and when they're moving.

It's not because we want to communicate our love to them. It's entirely one-sided. We watch simply because we like them. Babies and children are watched this way. To varying degrees, lovers probably are too. The people we admire and follow are watched this way. It's purely selfish on the part of the watcher. We watch because it feels good.

Still, some people can't help searching for the reason. "But why do I like this?" If the searching is fun, then by all means, search away. But rather than hunting for reasons, you're better off just doing more watching.

I recently saw a documentary about Takeshi Yoro. When asked about his health, his illness, his thoughts on things, he answered every question carefully, choosing his words with care. But the times he seemed happiest, more than anything, were when he was making specimens of his beloved insects, or when he was with fellow insect enthusiasts. There was obviously no reason behind it, and he didn't seem to have any intention of putting those specimens to practical use.

How wonderful it is to have something you love. That's what a living person really wants to do, isn't it?

The ropes of "why" and "what for" may be binding us hand and foot without our knowing it.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Toys exist in this world, and there are people who never stop playing.
entry.summary: The essay celebrates the profound joy found in simply watching and loving something without needing to justify, analyze, or derive purpose from it—a freedom from the constraints of rationalization that allows us to fully experience what brings us happiness.

entry.title: When you get old, it's something you should keep in mind.
entry.content:
Maybe this has already been said somewhere, but I really don't like the idea of being called a "toxic old person." There was a time when I was the young upstart, the junior nobody, but I hardly had anyone around me who struck me as that kind of old person. Sure, sometimes you'd see a stranger acting that way in a shop, or hear about examples in other people's stories. But among the people I actually worked with or saw regularly, there was truly no one who made me think, "Now there's a toxic old person." The older people I knew, and the seasoned figures from other circles, all of them listened well to what others had to say. None of them forced their fixed ideas on anyone. They never lectured or scolded, and they never went on about their own accomplishments. The seniors I respected stayed humble no matter how old they got, and I think they were also very good at the art of forgiveness. In TV dramas, you always see the stereotypical overbearing elder wielding power and keeping people down, but the fact that I managed to live so long without really encountering anyone like that in real life may simply mean I've been incredibly lucky.

Having seen so many wonderful older people, I naturally came to feel that I wanted to be like them someday. Even so, I want to say this: I do quietly worry that if I let my guard down, I might become that kind of person myself.

I was once taught that men, if they aren't constantly careful, will inevitably turn stingy and lecherous. And I've spent the last twenty years or so wondering whether, on top of those, getting older means you also risk becoming a nuisance. When people want to attack someone older, all they have to do is call them a "toxic old person" and it sounds perfectly justified. Even the admirable seniors I've known have all had it said about them at one time or another. And sure, they were, each of them, undeniably old.

For now, as far as I can tell about myself, I still find younger people fascinating and worthy of respect, so I think I'm probably okay. But who knows.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. You know how we talk about harmful insects and beneficial insects? I'd rather be "beneficially old" than "toxically old."
entry.summary: The author reflects on his fear of becoming a "toxic old person" despite having been surrounded by humble, respectful elders who served as positive role models, recognizing that even good people can be labeled this way and that maintaining openness to younger generations is essential to avoiding this fate.

entry.title: On the index and ring toes, among other things.
entry.content:
Every few years, I end up buying five-toe socks again. If I truly thought they were great, I'd wear them all the time. If I decided they were no good, I'd never put them on again. But somehow, after a few years pass, I find myself reaching for them once more. They're supposed to be good for you, after all. This time around, what got me started was learning a new way to put them on. Fitting each of the five toes into its little slot one by one had always been such a hassle, but the method I picked up this time makes it go relatively smoothly.

That aside, the thing that actually bothers me is what the toes are called. The big one on the inside, we call the "thumb" of the foot. And the little one on the outside is the "pinky." No argument there, no doubts whatsoever. Then the third one from the thumb, right in the middle, that's the "middle finger." Also fine. It's in the middle, so of course it's the middle finger.

But what about the one next to the thumb? Can we really call it the "pointer finger"? For a hand, sure, that makes sense. But this is a foot. Has anyone ever pointed at something with it, even once? Nobody's ever thrust it triumphantly toward the sky shouting "Number one!" either. It's a foot, after all.

And then there's one more: that hopelessly clumsy-looking fourth toe, wedged between the middle toe and the pinky. Are we really going to call that the "ring finger" with a straight face? Are you going to dab a bit of medicine on the tip of it and give it a lick? No. Nobody is doing that with that toe.

Anyway, while pondering all of this, here's the method for putting on five-toe socks. First, you pull the sock onto your "thumb," and in the same motion, slip it over the toe next to it. Then you jump straight to the unmistakable pinky. Once those three are locked in place, all doubt and confusion vanish. You just calmly fit the remaining two toes in. In numbers, it goes: 1, 2... 5, then 3, 4. Learning this technique has made the whole process so much more relaxed. Though, that said, I'll probably get bored and quit wearing them again before long.

When it comes to "things that seem vaguely good for your health," I'm the kind of person who can't help jumping on every one. Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. I don't usually mention it to people, but I'm always trying out supplements and things like that too.
entry.summary: The author explores the absurdity of calling foot toes by hand finger names while sharing a newfound technique that makes wearing five-toe socks less frustrating, ultimately reflecting on their cyclical relationship with health trends.

entry.title: Importing foreign culture and exporting culture abroad.
entry.content:
I think that's what I myself have been doing all along: living within Japan, crafting expressions meant to reach people who use the Japanese language, sharing a culture that anyone raised in Japan more or less understands. And it's not just me. I think there are many people like that. People have been talking about "internationalization" for ages, but even just the people inside Japan add up to a considerable number. In other words, Japan on its own is a reliable "market."

To the extent I've thought about other countries at all, I think it's been more about importing than exporting. Music, most of all, right? I never really listened to traditional Japanese folk music. Instead, we all shared in the fun-sounding music coming from abroad, and we loved it together. The same goes for art. Japanese-style painting was actually the rarity. We were educated in the history and aesthetics of Western art even at school, and the design all around us in the streets bore the influence of other countries. I mean, of course it did. We go through our days wearing Western clothes.

We imported foreign culture, absorbed it, and then expressed it within Japan's reasonably large "market." That's what we've been doing all along, isn't it? The audience was always, first and foremost, Japanese people. Music, painting, film, theater, manga, literature: almost all of it was fundamentally created with that in mind. You might say that creative expression has always been directed at people who already get it, people who share the same frame of reference.

And every now and then, some portion of that work would, to everyone's delight and surprise, turn out to resonate with people abroad too, and it would succeed as an export. Winning a foreign prize, or unexpectedly selling well somewhere overseas, that sort of thing.

But at some point, though I can't pinpoint exactly when, we began exporting to people in other parts of the world who speak different languages and were raised in different cultures. In some ways this happened naturally, in other ways quite deliberately. And I think there are things to be gained from this, and things to be lost. Both, without question. How are we going to live through this era where "both" is very much the case?

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. It seems to me there are plenty of hints out there, even right now.
entry.summary: The author reflects on how Japanese creators have traditionally made work for domestic audiences who share cultural references, but must now navigate the complex reality of simultaneously creating for both Japanese and international audiences—gaining global reach while potentially losing cultural specificity in the process.

entry.title: Fried rice is egg on rice.
entry.content:
Lately, I've been eating a lot of fried rice at old-school Chinese joints around town. It's not so much that I love fried rice or have a craving for it. It's more that I find myself genuinely impressed, all over again, by how its popularity never seems to fade. I've developed a serious interest. Like going to see a veteran singer who's been delivering great performances for years, I order the fried rice and savor it from every angle.

While I sit there eating my fried rice with this slightly sideways fascination, two more customers who came in after me have already ordered, without a moment's hesitation, "Fried rice!" One after the other.

I'd always assumed fried rice was something you ordered as a finishing carb at the end of a meal. But it turns out a meal of nothing but fried rice is perfectly complete. After all, it usually comes with a small bowl of soup. There's pickled red ginger, or zha cai, or some kind of pickle served alongside. It varies from place to place, but the main protein is usually roast pork. And it goes without saying that green onion is the ingredient doing all the behind-the-scenes work, pulling together the aroma, the flavor, and the texture to make that pork shine. If there's no green onion, I'd rather skip the fried rice that day. Bits of naruto fish cake? You can leave them out, and honestly it's better without. But green onion is absolutely essential. Borrow some from the restaurant next door if you have to.

So what is fried rice, at its core? It's rice. And really, it's egg-on-rice. Fried rice is stir-fried egg-on-rice. You scatter green onion over it as a seasoning and mix in some roast pork. Egg-on-rice is already a meal that stands on its own. When all else fails, egg-on-rice will get you through. How much more so, then, with fried rice. The reason fried rice never loses its popularity is that, at heart, it's egg-on-rice.

Incidentally, I have invented something I call "ramen fried rice." You stir-fry rice with egg, then add roast pork, chopped menma bamboo shoots, and green onion. Season it with the sauce from the roast pork, and you'd do well to add some pepper too. That's it.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. If anyone knows a neighborhood Chinese place that puts menma in their fried rice, I'd love to go.
entry.summary: The author discovers that fried rice's enduring popularity stems from its essential simplicity—at its core, it's just seasoned egg and rice with a few key ingredients like green onion and roast pork, making it a complete and satisfying meal that never goes out of style.

entry.title: The world is full of art.
entry.content:
Okamoto Taro, when asked "What is art?", answered: it's that feeling of "What on earth is this?!" I've always loved that answer, and I've kept it tucked away in a corner of my mind.

There are so many "What on earth is this?!" moments. For a newborn baby, everything it sees, hears, and touches must be one big "What on earth is this?!" Anything that lies beyond what you can imagine, anything not yet named, all of it is "What on earth is this?!"

In other words, the moment a person feels "What on earth is this?!", their world has expanded by exactly that much. And that, Okamoto Taro went ahead and brilliantly declared, is the answer to "What is art?"

If "What on earth is this?!" is what art is, then nearly everything people have ever wondered about, asking "Is that art? Is this art?", ends up being art. Naturally, some people will object. Especially those who believe art must carry "a value deserving of reverence" or "commercial value." If anything and everything gets recognized as art, that's a real problem for them.

But let me say this on Okamoto Taro's behalf (forgive me!): what if we simply accepted that there can be "art that doesn't deserve reverence" and "art with no commercial value"? Wouldn't that settle it?

From ancient times right up to our present age of AI, the popular music that people poured their hearts into creating, the packaging design on everyday products, all of it is art. Just like grand temple architecture or portraits of kings, a great rock song, a gymnast's routine, the laughter drawn out by a comedy act, anything that makes someone feel "What on earth is this?!" is art. And among all of it, some things will endure for a thousand years.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. Even an amateur's karaoke is art. Not that anyone's going to praise it.
entry.summary: Art is fundamentally that feeling of wonder and discovery—the moment something expands our understanding of the world—making nearly everything humans create potentially worthy of the title, whether it's a grand temple or an amateur's karaoke performance.

entry.title: Being connected, and being alone.
entry.content:
The title of yesterday's piece was "I'm sure it'll be: 'That was great! That was great!'" No matter how the soccer match against Brazil turned out, I wanted to be able to say "That was great! That was great!" It wasn't meant as a prediction. I wrote it as a wish. There's maybe a touch of putting on a brave face in there, but I think "That was great! That was great!" was the right call.

Why is it so much fun, all of us watching a live sporting event together? I imagine it's because you can feel that you're "connected to so many people," that "so many people are feeling the same way you are." Especially late at night, when you'd normally be alone in your everyday life, there's something deeply satisfying about feeling that "all these people are connected to me right now." If you can think, "Look how many of us there are," it gives you courage just to be alive. Maybe that's how alone we usually are.

Being in a crowd at a live concert, attending a rally where everyone shares the same conviction, being in the middle of a fireworks show or a festival. We play it cool and say things like, "Sure is crowded, huh," but we went out of our way to be there, so clearly we don't mind. Just knowing that there are this many people like you, I think it awakens some very primal pleasure.

I talk about this as though I'm just an observer, but I do love "being connected to lots of people." Sometimes people tease me, calling me "Mr. Majority." And yet, honestly, it's a little more complicated than that. In those moments of "being connected to lots of people," I sometimes get wrapped in a different kind of loneliness, not the same as when I'm truly alone. I'm not sure how to put it. It's like wanting to let go of the hands I'm holding in this enormous human chain, just for a moment. Like wanting to test whether I could let go anytime I wanted.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. People's feelings really do come as one, front and back inseparable.
entry.summary: The essay explores how shared experiences at live events create a profound sense of human connection that temporarily alleviates our fundamental loneliness, while acknowledging the paradoxical desire to occasionally escape even that collective togetherness. Despite finding deep satisfaction in feeling "connected to so many people," the author recognizes a more complex emotional reality—a simultaneous need for individual autonomy within the crowd.

entry.title: Surely, "thank goodness! Thank goodness!"
entry.content:
Once again, I'm writing this a little earlier than usual. For the soccer, of course. Kickoff is at 2 a.m., and I'm normally still awake at that hour anyway. In that sense, staying up isn't even a question. It's right in my sweet spot. But that alone doesn't solve the problem. Staying awake is easy enough, but if I'm glued to a soccer match until four in the morning, there's no time left to write this column. So I had a choice: either work myself into a fervor and write after 4 a.m., or get the column done before the match starts. One or the other.

This column might look like breezy, throwaway writing, but it's never as easy as it seems once you actually sit down to do it. Sometimes I'll try dashing one off in twenty minutes, just to see if I can speed-write it, but I end up flustered, and I suppose I just don't have the natural talent for it, so I give up with nothing to show. I'd rather not struggle with anything besides soccer in the early morning hours, so I decided to write this in advance.

Once I'd made that decision, I figured I might as well take my bath early too. Lately I've been trying to bathe early and get to bed early. It's not as easy as it sounds, but I'm doing my best. So here I am, already bathed before 10 p.m. My body is nice and warm. And since I want to be ready to sleep the moment I feel like it, I've already brushed my teeth and put on my pajamas. I check the clock: 11:17 p.m. And this column, as you can see, is already about eighty percent done.

All I had to do was grumble and gripe a little, and the thing practically wrote itself. How convenient is that? I can't exactly pull this trick every day, but this time, it really saved me.

Come to think of it, this column goes up at eleven. So the result of "that match" will be out for all to see by then, like it or not.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. Who knew a mere ten-hour time difference could hold so much depth.
entry.summary: The author discovers that by writing about the constraints of his schedule—staying up for an early morning soccer match while meeting a column deadline—he inadvertently solves the problem itself, finding that honest reflection on life's small challenges can be both authentic and effortless.

entry.title: Looking at a teddy bear, lost in thought.
entry.content:
"If you hate the monk, you'll hate his robe too." It's a very well-known proverb. The idea is that once you've decided you dislike a monk, even the robe he wears starts to seem hateful. It's a quirk of human psychology. When you think of it as being literally about monks and robes, it might not feel especially relevant to your own life, but really, most of us do this kind of thing all the time. You hate someone, so you hate their entire profession. You can't stand a certain person, so you can't stand the things they like. You dislike a team, so you despise their mascot. We slip into these feelings without even thinking about it. (And of course, it works in reverse too: you love someone, and suddenly their favorite food becomes your favorite food.)

Still, I think what matters is being aware of this tendency, this habitual quirk of human psychology, and learning to keep it in check. That's what I believe, anyway. But the world doesn't work that neatly. Once you've labeled someone the "monk" (i.e., the enemy), the people around them start to look like enemies too, and everything associated with them gets treated as the "robe" (i.e., the enemy's weapon). Whatever the "monk" says must be a lie, all of it. Human history has been doing this sort of thing forever. Maybe it was even worse in the past than it is now. Then again, things have been pretty rough in the age of the internet, too.

But then, you don't hear about anyone who's afraid of bears, who hates bears, saying they also hate teddy bears or Winnie-the-Pooh. I've never met anyone who's furious at Iran and has therefore developed a grudge against Persian rugs. Outside of a certain handful of, shall we say, very intense people, most of us are actually fairly level-headed about these things.

For what it's worth, I'm allergic to shrimp and crab, but I don't hate them and I'm not angry at them. If I'm reborn in another life, I want to eat all the shrimp and crab I can get.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. I'm not a Hanshin Tigers fan, but I still wear Onitsuka Tigers.
entry.summary: The essay explores how we habitually transfer our dislike of people onto everything associated with them, while acknowledging that this psychological tendency, though real and historically consequential, is often overstated—most people maintain more nuance and separation in their judgments than the proverb suggests.

entry.title: It's Saturday, but I went to Kanda for tonkatsu.
entry.content:
When the tonkatsu arrives at the table, right there in front of me, it always begins with the same thought: it's still so hot. I separate three slices from the left side of the cutlet, pulling them slightly away from the rest. A light squeeze of lemon, just a whisper of it, then soy sauce dripped on carefully so as not to use too much. The hottest, freshest pieces I eat this way. Oh, and a dab of mustard too, I almost forgot. My front teeth bite through the crust, and it shatters gently, like a thin sheet of ice. Then the meat, the pork, is instantly converted into pure deliciousness and spreads across my mouth. The aroma, the flavor, the perfect tenderness, all of it touching everywhere, the top of my tongue, the insides of my cheeks, and the story of the tonkatsu begins to unfold. I chew with my back teeth, savoring again, jaw and tongue moving as desire commands. More chewing, swallowing, biting down harder. When I really press into it, I can feel the rich fat seeping out.

I deliberately avoided inspecting the cross-section too closely, but the first two slices or so from the left side are the fattiest part, and that fat is precisely what makes the "special loin" worth boasting about. While my tongue registers the lush, spreading richness of the fat, I take a bite of rice. The harmony of tonkatsu fat and plain white rice plays like music inside my mouth.

Tonkatsu sauce comes after that. Here, too, I'm careful not to overdo it, applying the sauce one slice at a time, adding mustard, savoring the texture of the crust against my teeth. By this point I've settled in, and the pace of my chewing slows. I lift my gaze slightly and think: this is so good, so good again today. When I'm eating at the counter and happen to catch the eye of the owner who does the frying, I give a small nod. The cabbage, the miso soup, the pickled daikon and lightly salted pickles, the rice. I give thanks to all the supporting players that make the tonkatsu taste even better.

When the plate is empty, it's time for the check. This is a place I know well, so I say what I feel, simply and sincerely. "That was truly delicious." I say it twice, and bow.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. I'd already decided on Friday that Saturday would be a tonkatsu day.
entry.summary: The essay is a meditative celebration of eating tonkatsu, exploring how mindful attention to each sensory detail—the crackle of the crust, the richness of the fat, the harmony with rice—transforms a simple meal into a profound experience of gratitude and joy. Through ritualized enjoyment and respect for both the food and those who prepared it, the narrator reveals that true satisfaction comes not from the food itself, but from the deliberate presence brought to its consumption.

entry.title: How far down do you eat a watermelon?
entry.content:
I'm pretty fond of watermelon, and I eat it often. At some point I realized that, somewhere along the way into adulthood, I'd stopped eating the part near the rind where it isn't sweet anymore. I don't scrape at the white part, of course, but even within the red part, I leave behind whatever isn't sweet.

If the childhood version of me saw this, I wonder what he'd think. "There's still good watermelon left!" he'd surely say. Because that kid ate every bit of it. I still remember the feeling. The red part is edible, and it should be eaten. The proudest way to eat watermelon was to keep going until not a trace of red remained. Who exactly I was showing off for, I'm not sure.

But the truth is, the part near the rind just doesn't taste good. Maybe some people like it, but it's certainly not sweet. As a kid, it didn't matter, but as an adult, I came to feel that forcing yourself to eat the parts you don't enjoy actually ruins the memory of all the delicious bites that came before. You've been savoring this sweet, wonderful watermelon, and then the grand finale is bland, tasteless rind? That felt wrong.

At some point, without quite realizing it, I started thinking this way, and I changed how I ate watermelon. It's not a matter of "eat the red, leave the white." That's not how I see it anymore. It's about sweetness, not color. I made that decision for myself.

The same thing applies to melon, if I'm being honest. With melon, the colors are less distinct, so I just stop at the point where I judge it's no longer sweet.

I've never looked into how far other people eat their watermelon or melon, so I couldn't say. And nobody's ever scolded me for leaving red watermelon behind. While I'm at it, I should mention that I stopped drinking ramen broth to the last drop a long time ago, even though the broth is the most expensive part of the bowl. I imagine there are people who'd have something to say about that.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. Whether this makes me stingy or extravagant, I honestly can't tell myself.
entry.summary: As we mature, we learn to prioritize quality and enjoyment over wasteful obligation, choosing to savor only what truly satisfies us rather than forcing ourselves through the bland remnants simply because they're there.

entry.title: I want to learn as if I were playing.
entry.content:
Learning something and playing at something are probably close to the same thing. Learning tennis, learning shogi, learning to swim, learning to dance. When the time spent learning feels enjoyable, it becomes play. But the moment learning becomes "for the sake of" something, it turns into obligation, and it's no longer play. It makes an enormous difference what the person doing it, the one at the center of it, thinks about what they're doing. You could say it all comes down to a difference in mindset. If you go in thinking, "I don't want to do this, but I have to," it will reliably become tedious.

Even when something is tedious, you can still improve, and I know there are times when you feel a sense of accomplishment and think, "I'm so glad, that makes me happy." But when there's a good teacher or a good mentor who can make learning feel enjoyable, the learner's mindset gets set to "this seems fun." Once that happens, the time itself becomes something you spend thinking "this is fun," and even a bit of hardship or difficulty becomes just a dash of spice within the play.

Learning from someone who teaches well, as though you're just playing together. I never thought such a thing was possible. And yet, it seems like this wonderful thing is happening in all sorts of learning situations these days. I grew up in an era when learning meant strictness, the occasional scolding, and the promise that mastery lay on the other side of suffering. That was just how it was, so I hated lessons. I'm incredibly envious of people who get to learn in a way that's enjoyable.

I imagine the theories and methods on the other side of learning, the "teaching" side, have been advancing too. Learning that feels like playing. Even now, even at this late stage, I'd love to try something like that. Age shouldn't matter.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Come to think of it, someone I know was saying they want to learn mahjong.
entry.summary: The essay argues that learning becomes play when it's intrinsically enjoyable rather than pursued as an obligation, and that skilled teachers can transform the learning experience by fostering genuine curiosity and fun rather than relying on discipline and suffering. The author reflects on how modern teaching methods have evolved to make learning feel like play, something they wish they'd experienced growing up.

entry.title: What should you do with your lives?
entry.content:
If a young person asked me, "What should I do with my life?" would I be able to answer? I've been turning this over in my mind for a while now. Come to think of it, I never asked anyone that question myself. Back then, all I was really thinking about was whether I could survive. Even the idea of "finding happiness" felt like an aspiration that didn't suit someone like me. And yet, strangely, I wasn't much of a worrier in those days.

But setting myself aside. When a young person wants to know, "What should I be doing?" I can understand that feeling. It probably comes from a place of, "If I just knew what it was, I'd be ready to work hard." Like saying, "Give me the problem, and I'll do everything I can to solve it."

The truth is, finding the problem is itself the problem, and the answer to that problem is simply discovering what problem you need to solve. But that's a question that spans an entire lifetime, so saying as much doesn't really help.

I want to think about this for my own sake, too. What should young people be doing?

When I search my memory, the mentors in my life all said pretty much the same thing. "Read the classics." It may have been a kind of stock answer. There was also, "Get your heart broken, many times over." You could say these are harmless, inoffensive pieces of advice.

But compared to the sort of guidance that came along in a more recent era, things like "Get an MBA" or "Build your network" or "Study computers," advice that sounds so eminently practical, I don't think those older words are the least bit inferior. "Read the classics" and "Get your heart broken" both seem to be ways of saying, "Try to understand what it means to be human."

Well, I'll keep thinking about it.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. "What should I be doing?" It's a question that grown-ups and the elderly are still asking, too.
entry.summary: Rather than seeking practical advice for finding purpose, young people should focus on understanding what it means to be human through experiences like reading classics and emotional growth, questions that adults grapple with throughout their entire lives.

entry.title: I want to bring the hot spring version of me home.
entry.content:
For as long as I can remember, I took it as a given that baths were something you took before bed. Work, reading, watching shows, whatever it was, I'd finish everything I wanted to do first, and only when there was nothing left would I go to sleep. I'd kept that routine for decades. So the bath had to come right before bed. It seems bathing was never something I actually wanted to do. Of course it felt good once I got in the water. And keeping your body clean isn't a bad thing. But somehow I'd always thought of it as more of an obligation. So I never really lingered or took my time. I was sleepy, after all. Once I was in the bath, all I wanted was to hurry up and get to bed. It was a stretch of time that pretty much symbolized how little breathing room I had in my life.

But when I go to a hot spring? Completely different person. I'm Mr. Relaxation, king of the bathhouse, lord of the outdoor pool. My mood is expansive, I'll soak again and again, I get all warm and toasty, and I'm savoring every moment. Work can wait, I'll get to it later. The point is to let the hot spring wash away every last bit of fatigue and worry, to sigh as much as I please, and that's perfectly fine. I become an utterly self-indulgent hot spring creature. Isn't it too different from my daily bath at home? It's way too different.

So I thought: I want to bring that hot-spring version of me back to my own house. First, the water temperature at home might have been a touch too lukewarm. Time for a fresh start, turn the heat up a notch. And on top of that, I'd use the Nemukuma Forest bath salts to get those hot-spring minerals in there. Just that alone made a world of difference in how warm and cozy it felt. And then I started actually wanting to enjoy my bath. I thought long and hard about how to make that happen, and I came to the conclusion that "right before bed," the way I'd done it for decades, just wasn't going to work. There was simply no room to breathe.

(A few days of trial and error followed around this point...)

When I switched to bathing around nine or ten at night, somehow it started feeling remarkably like a hot spring. I can even still get work done afterward! And more than anything, I've been able to get to bed a little earlier.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. Of course, when it comes to tinkering with your daily habits, it's never easy to make things stick.
entry.summary: The author discovered that by shifting their bath time earlier in the evening and enhancing the bathing experience with better water temperature and bath salts, they transformed bathing from a rushed obligation into a genuinely enjoyable ritual that finally brought the relaxation they'd always felt at hot springs into their own home.

entry.title: It's only after your ignorance is exposed that the real challenge begins.
entry.content:
Over the course of a lifetime, we come into contact with an enormous number of people. We do things together, we observe each other. So many people share the same time, the same spaces with us. Family, friends, people we play with or work alongside, the staff at places we frequent, our customers.

Some we've known since childhood. Others we met during those brash, restless years of adolescence or early adulthood. There are people we fell in love with, and people in front of whom we embarrassed ourselves.

No matter how much you try to make yourself look impressive, no matter how much you dress things up, no matter how wise or kind you try to appear, the people who've been around you for a long time pretty much see through it. Your failings, your flaws, your selfishness, your dishonesty, your weaknesses. They end up knowing nearly all of it. When people have been watching you up close for years, there's simply nowhere to hide. Even those who say the most admirable things are often quite different at home.

When you've met enough people, spent time with enough people, done enough things, talked enough, eventually people come to understand you so thoroughly that you start to think, "Well, there's probably nothing left they haven't figured out." In other words, if you live long enough, people have long since figured out that you're a fool.

As you've probably guessed, I'm talking about myself here. Especially after all these years of sitting down day after day, groaning my way through even these short pieces of writing. No matter how I try to put on a good face, it's perfectly clear by now that I'm nothing all that special.

I'm not sure exactly when I realized this. But at some point, without quite noticing, I gave something up, and I arrived at this way of thinking: life really begins after everyone's figured out you're a fool.

Of course, it's not as though I've lost all vanity or desire. But I do feel I've made a fair amount of peace with it. And that, I think, makes it easier to be free. You know?

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. Well, I suppose I've spent most of this long journey in my everyday clothes anyway.
entry.summary: The essay argues that true freedom and peace come from accepting that those close to us inevitably see through our pretenses and flaws, allowing us to stop performing and simply be ourselves. Once we surrender the exhausting effort to appear impressive, life becomes easier and more authentic.
